comments.content:
Good Evening!

First off, thanks very much to JMG and those of the commentariat who advised me on how best to meditate on the philosophical triads from The Dolmen Arch, Volume I last week: my meditations on these went much smoother, and I felt a lot less like I was bashing my head against a wall.

Secondly, thanks for sharing the photo and the information on Dr. Ashley - I'll be quite interested to see where you go from here in tracing your occult lineages!

To Share: I've kept on with taking Christian prayers and working out a Heathen take on them by writing a "Hail Idun" prayer. Idun/Idunna/Idhun doesn't get as much attention as some of the other Gods and Goddesses in the Germanic pantheon, but she's been important in my own experience, and I suspect that she'll keep on being near the heart of my work to come up with a "Heathen Rosary".

To Ask: I'm afraid I can't find the link easily, but sometime over the past month or two, you made a comment either here or on the main blog about the difference between worshipping the pre-Ragnarok Gods and the post-Ragnarok Gods, and I was hoping you might expand a bit on your thoughts about both this specific case, as well as the broader idea of the relationship of worship/spiritual practice with notions of "mythic time" - if myths are stories that are "true, but never were", how does that relate to placing different emphasis on different "times" in myths? If this is overly broad, I can, of course, come up with a more specific question.

As always, thanks so much JMG for hosting this space, and thank you to everyone here for sharing, commenting, and creating a wonderful community I feel lucky to consider myself a part of.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I can't find that link either, and I'm startled to hear that I've been pontificating about that subject -- as I'm not a heathen and have only a basic acquaintance with the Eddas et al., I try not to make a fool of myself by making claims about religions I don't belong to. In broader terms, the relationship of myth and time is complex; it's clear that in a good many pantheons there are gods who are no longer worshipped, who have retired or even died, and in some (the Norse tradition is an example) there's a very clear sense that the current set of gods won't be around indefinitely. Myths never happened but always are, but it's by no means clear to me whether or not the sense of one set of gods being replaced by another is a myth or a reflection of religious experience.

1) Hmm! That sounds like a lot of fun. I don't know of any other examples, but it's a genre that could use many more additions.

2) The four royal stars are four very bright stars chosen millennia ago to track the position of the Sun at the times of the solstices and equinoxes. comments.title: A reply from the lake and forests
comments.content:
You advised me to listen to the local nature spirits. The one time I got a clear answer it was a feeling of "Love us, rather than focus on what you want from us." This was when I would focus on the question of what they wanted me to do in regards to the possibility of many people moving here in the future.

Most people say they love nature but it comes with a caveat. They view nature as a resource they can use, "What can you do for me?" I love nature but I still want my house, yard, car, superstore, and all the infrastructure that requires.

Tragically I think the forests here are done for. As the population expands we aren't getting denser. New development chops everything down for box stores and suburbs. At least I live in the old de-industrial area crowded next to the lake, with "missing middle": apartments, multiplexes, cottages, townhouses/rowhouses, etc.

1. How do you spiritually deal with witnessing environmental destruction? I ignore most media, but what if it's out on my daily walk?

2. How do you recommend clearing your head to be able to hear local nature spirits? I currently do my pre-meditation techniques by the lake: color breathing, affirmations, singing hymns, then I focus on the lake and just walk along it and listen. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: A reply from the lake and forests
replies.content:
1) Robinson Jeffers is my guide here: harsh but essential. Here's his poem "Carmel Point":

"The extraordinary patience of things!
This beautiful place defaced with a crop of suburban houses —
How beautiful when we first beheld it,
Unbroken field of poppy and lupin walled with clean cliffs;
No intrusion but two or three horses pasturing,
Or a few milch cows rubbing their flanks on the outcrop rockheads —
Now the spoiler has come: does it care?
Not faintly. It has all time. It knows the people are a tide
That swells and in time will ebb, and all
Their works dissolve. Meanwhile the image of the pristine beauty
Lives in the very grain of the granite,
Safe as the endless ocean that climbs our cliff. — As for us:
We must uncenter our minds from ourselves;
We must unhumanize our views a little, and become confident
As the rock and ocean that we were made from."

2) Many years of meditation practice make it easy for me to clear my thoughts away whenever I choose to. Back before that was the case, I'd find a place to sit and do fourfold breath for a while, and enter into a meditative state.

replies.title: Re: A reply from the lake and forests
replies.content: That's a wonderful poem, thanks!

replies.title: Re: A reply from the lake and forests
replies.content: Not the OP. Thanks for the Carmel Point poem by Jeffers. It resonates with me especially right now, a very rich new neighbour bought some wild woodland next to us, cut a bunch of trees down last week, and filled a small but vibrant wetland. All week, as truckload after truckload of fill rolled by, I had a queasy, ugly feeling in my gut. That is to say, I'm very glad to read that poem.

replies.title: Re: A reply from the lake and forests
replies.content:
Another Jeffers:

These grand and fatal movements toward death: the grandeur
of the mass
Makes pity a fool, the tearing pity
For the atoms of the mass, the persons, the victims, makes it
seem monstrous
To admire the tragic beauty they build.
It is beautiful as a river flowing or a slowly gathering
Glacier on a high mountain rock-face,
Bound to plow down a forest, or as frost in November,
The gold and flaming death-dance for leaves,
Or a girl in the night of her spent maidenhood, bleeding and
kissing.
I would burn my right hand in a slow fire
To change the future ... I should do foolishly. The beauty
of modern
Man is not in the persons but in the
Disastrous rhythm, the heavy and mobile masses, the dance of the
Dream-led masses down the dark mountain.

1: Recognize what's beyond your ability to affect and what you can do something about. (a lot getting clear-cut in preparation for a house going up as opposed to a potentially harmful business being proposed). Just a few years ago people in my hometown successfully fought off an attempt to build a lithium battery factory with its attendant environmental hazards. The Northern Pass Project was also successfully scotched by residents of the northern half of the state who weren't too keen on 100 plus foot tall electrical towers marching across the landscape. Money fuels these projects and I think time is running out for all of it. It won't last much longer. Not a big collapse, just everything rapidly fizzling to a mere sputter.

And Nature isn't down and out yet. This morning I watched a red fox calmly trotting along headed somewhere right through my backyard (I live in town, not in the countryside). There's a tiny nature park nearby with a pond that a pair of Canada geese found congenial enough so they are raising a brood of goslings. Bumblebees and to a lesser extent fireflies have made a comeback. Yes, many things will be lost but there are plenty of other things waiting quietly in the background. Follow the advice of the nature spirits and follow their lead. They know how to be patient.

JLfromNH/Murky Gibbering Dragon

comments.title: Magical Aid
comments.content:
Hey JMG!
Thanks for making this space available. I’m fairly familiar with the Golden Dawn tradition, and I’ve had a friend who asked for some magical assistance with depression. I admit I’m not really sure what can be done to help that.
Is there any working you’d recommend or should I just point him in the direction of the OSA material and professional help?

replies.title: Re: Magical Aid
replies.content: I'd definitely recommend the OSA material and professional help, though he may have to do some looking to find a professional who will actually help him instead of just drugging him into numbness. That said, you can do quite a bit with magic. Begin by doing the Rose+Cross ritual while imagining your friend in the central space, and calling donw the white light onto him -- that's a good general healing and blessing, and can be repeated once a week with good effect. Beyond that, depression corresponds to Saturn among the planets, and so magical workings invoking Jupiter, Venus, and the Sun will counter it. If you've got a good basis in Golden Dawn magic, you can examine your friend's natal chart, figure out which of these three planets is weakest, and make a talisman of that planet for your friend to help him find a more balanced emotional state.

replies.title: Re: Magical Aid
replies.content:
A question, if I may, from somebody with no GD knowledge (not the OP):

Afaik, Christopher Warnock says that you shouldn‘t get any of his renaissance style planetary talismans for a planet which is afflicted in your chart. Do the Golden Dawn talismans work differently?

And: many thanks for the Robinson Jeffers poem further above. Great point of view, and beautiful poem! :-))

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Magical Aid
replies.content: Golden Dawn talismans work differently. They don't draw their energy from the heavens, so they aren't affected by astrological conditions in the same way.

replies.title: Re: Magical Aid
replies.content:
Pardon me for being a stickler, but a month ago you told me that "If you invoke a planetary energy it's always going to be colored by the condition of the planet in your natal chart." In the same thread, you recommended to another commenter whose natal chart planets are all afflicted that he avoid astrological magic altogether. From the context, I had assumed you were referring to Golden Dawn style planetary talismans, though I'm not so sure now.

Would you be so kind as to comment further on how GD planetary talismans are affected by astrological conditions, the magician's and/or user's natal chart in particular?

My understanding, such as it is, is that the GD talismans are basically a little piece of the magician's life force that is separated out and dedicated to a specific task. I was thinking that since my natal Mercury is afflicted and I have some difficulties with communication, that a Mercury talisman might not improve my communication skills, but instead bring me more opportunities for communication with the usual difficulties.

replies.title: Re: Magical Aid
replies.content:
Please reread my comment:

"Golden Dawn talismans work differently. They don't draw their energy from the heavens, so they aren't affected by astrological conditions in the same way."

That doesn't mean they aren't affected. It means that they're affected differently, and that Chris Warnock's rule -- which is entirely relevant to the kind of astrological talismans he makes and sells -- does not apply to them.

Golden Dawn talismans combine the energy of the practitioner with energies descending from the sephiroth of the Tree of Life, the eternal archetypes of the planets. They also have much more precise intentions. If you want to do a GD-style talisman to improve your communications skills, you can do that -- aiming it specifically at that goal -- if you have the GD magical chops.

replies.content: Thank you. I think I am beginning to understand (sometimes it takes me a while).

comments.title: Mistletoe conundrums

I need your help. Last new moon (5. August) I approached and oak asked for material for a wand. I was given a branch with a mistletoe plant on it. It even fell so, that it newer touched the ground. b.) to cut them in 2-3 inch pieces and reassemble and glue them back together in a straight stick.

All, are there any carpenters, or woodworkers, who could offer advice?

Best regards,
V

comments.title: Jupiter Synchronicities
comments.content:
So, I just wanted to thank anyone from the main blog who gave me some kind words of encouragement when I was doing badly a week or two ago; I am now feeling much better, due in part to the tale which I am about to tell!

I've been having some minor and major synchronicities, all involving Jupiter. First, when I was visiting my mom, she randomly showed me a picture of Jupiter with the clearest, sharpest image I've ever seen. I was impressed, and thought nothing of it.

A week later, I sank into a deep, pernicious depression, full of anger and bitterness. I essentially fell into a black hole of meaninglessness for a while, more intense than anything I've felt in the last decade, rivaled only by a spate when my health was failing right after I got out of college. I watched a random, supposedly shitty video, muttering about how dumb and pointless it was, until suddenly, I started grinning helplessly and felt enormously uplifted. In this weird guy, a person who plays, loves, and has mastered an obscure videogame, I saw a vortex of extraordinary meangingfulness; a sense that every one of his movements were imbued with unconscious intention that was more than it was, if that makes any sense. I looked up his birth chart on a whim, and saw that he was a Sagittarius, ruled by Jupiter. Okay, cool! I looked up another game player who I love from the same scene, also a Sag. Okay, so I like Sag; big deal. For some ineffable reason, I looked up random people who had the same "feel" as both of them, an extraordinary meanginfulness that transcended the logical, base natures of their actions. C.S Lewis sprung to mind, as I had recently finished the Space Trilogy; also a Sag. Winston Churchill, since I'd read a bio about him where, despite starting out biased, I had been utterly charmed; Sag. A political commentator and podcaster I found on this blog; Sag. My favorite fantasy author; Sag. The comedy personality I used to worship; Sag. This continued for about 10 times in a row, and was fascinating. I was a little spooked, then shelved it.

Later that night, I was reading an analysis of Gemini-associated myths, as I am a Gemini, and came across a little tidbit. Sagittarius, apparently, being opposite to Gemini, rules MEANING as opposed to endless bits of information. Immediately I was struck; hope, faith, exuberance, generosity, philosophy, religion ... all of these Jupiter ruled phenomena are intensely meaningful by their very nature. As opposed to the dissolving, vivid, nonself mysticism of Neptune, Jupiter's spirituality rules meaning; Tarnas says that Jupiter CROWNS whatever it is associated with, makes it royal and regal. I was struck once again, and slightly unnerved.

A couple of days later, I went to the library and grabbed a couple of random scifi books from an authors that I find ambiguous yet interesting. I didnt look at the cover or actually try and see what it was about. I start reading. The main character, a historical figure, is doing a horoscope in the first third of the book where Jupiter is in THE SAME POSITION AS MY OWN BIRTH CHART. Whoah, okay. Later in the book, the character FLIES UP TO JUPITER IN A SPACESHIP and then COMMUNES WITH THE PLANETARY SPIRIT OF JUPITER, proving that astrology is real. By this point my eyes are bulging out of my skull. I close the book and look at the front cover; a huge picture of Jupiter. I look at the back of the book; another huge picture of Jupiter. At this point, I remember some of the other stuff, and I realize what is going on, numbskull distracted Gemini that I can be. Jung was, as usual, right as rain and just as refreshing.

So, um ... it seems like Jupiter is reaching out to me, right? I have bought a blue candle, and I intend to light it on the new moon, as that is apparently when you want to invoke new influences rather than banish bad ones. My questions are related to this; should I say some sort of thanks or invocation? Is it rude to ask for anything as you normally do with a candle, should I just thank Jupiter for his generous munificence and leave it at that, since he has so obviously helped me? Is this a sign that I should go in for full scale worship of Jupiter? (he is in a very prominent placement in my birth chart, though he is overall in mixed dignity.) Lastly, I would be grateful to hear any further thoughts about what this might mean or what actions I should take in response from JMG or the commentariat. I am delighted, and a little spooked trying to figure out what this means and what to do.


(Lastly as a comment, wow that Libra Ingress prediction. Buying more food and ramping up other preparations, stat. Thank goodness for the Uranus-Venus trine, as I'm not sure if I would bear such calamity well if not for the hope of renewal afterwards.)

-Derpherder

replies.title: Re: Jupiter Synchronicities
replies.content:
Fascinating. I think Jupiter is definitely saying hi. In your place I'd burn a candle on Thursdays and recite the Orphic prayer to Jupiter, though of course your mileage may vary. You might consider praying to him and asking him to guide you to what you should do in response.

What was the SF novel? That sounds fun.

replies.title: Re: Jupiter Synchronicities
replies.content:
Thanks JMG! It was Galileo's Dream by Kim Stanley Robinson. Overall I found the book interesting but frustrating, as it was historical fiction/scifi alternating to a striking, but odd effect. Robinson is weird; he has an enormous faith in science, but in the book's scifi sections, focus on science made the world into a miserable, traumatized place for a literal millennium before they accidentally contact the spirit of Jupiter. The book is also very philosophic and explores the proper relationship between science and religion (Galileo mentions himself being Mercury dominant, so this clash is entirely astrologically appropriate, funnily enough.) He feels like a guy who cant let go of the myth of progress but longs for more, especially since most of his books feature a spiritual tradition (in this one astrology, in another Tibetan ideas about reincarnation) being literally true alongside a bizarre amount of science and progress triumphalism.

As for the candle, thank you for the advice, this seems eminently sensible! I will try this Thursday and report back with results! Very excited!

-Derpherder

comments.title: Meditation colours
comments.content:
So, like many of us here I've had some setbacks in my life over the past couple of years. I've even had a few extra non-pandemic ones for good measure. And I've developed this technique where I go away by myself for a couple of days and meditate until I'm 100% convinced that my physical body is just an energy cloud pulsating in a bigger energy field. When I can stay in that state for a while I find I can usually sort of pull a solution to the latest setback out of the quantum cloud, or whatever you might call it.

So I've had another setback, and I followed my technique again, and a good solution seems to have appeared right on cue. But that's not the question. When I've done this sort of meditation before it's been either completely black out in the energy field, or maybe it's just a little bit grey, as if there's a feeble little light somewhere. But this time I started seeing what looks like some sort of pipe that's electric blue. It's really indescribably beautiful. If that colour existed in our physical reality I would probably spend all my time chasing it down, collecting it, ruining my life over it, etc. Any idea what I'm seeing?

This is something about magic that has been happening in my life. I don't have a question. I just wanted to share it here.

Some context: I belong to a south asian culture (in a south asian country), where multiple generations live in the same household. The social mores are greatly different from anything you will see in today's USA. Also, occultism has been in my family for generations. comments.title: 4 questions

1) Do kisses (romantic, familial and friendly) have any effect on the etheric or astral levels? I assume something similar would be possible on the etheric and astral levels too, and why kissing the wrong someone can just feel 'off'?

2) Is there anything to birthstones, or is it just some nonsense made up by the gemstone industry? From your book on Natural Magic, gemstones are obviously in there with specific benefits, but what I'm trying to get at is whether:
a) birthstones have greater effect for some months than others (eg. a Gemini would get more benefit from a moonstone than, say a Taurus would)
b) they can be used to balance out the disadvantages of a certain birth sign (so the shifting, changeable nature of a pearl, moonstone or Alexandrite could counteract some of the mercurial nature of a Gemini - or would it make it worse?)
c) that they enhance the positive aspects of a given birth sign (so again, quick wittedness and adaptability for a Gemini... can you guess that I'm a Gemini?)
d) a bit of all of the above
e) there is no specific advantage to a given gemstone for a specific month beyond what any other month would obtain

...leaving aside the obvious fact that calendar birth months do not align neatly with astrological signs...

3) I think you've stated before that it's relatively common to be reincarned into a direct descendent. What happens if you have no relatives who survive you and carry on your family line? (So, say, you and your entire extended family die in an extremely unfortunate plane crash)

4) Resurrection, in the sense of reanimating a recently dead person, is reasonably common in the Bible and several other holy texts (Lazarus is the obvious one, but I think there are 4 others in the New Testament?). In the Bible, this mostly done by Jesus, but not exclusively, inplying that in theory 'anyone' could do it (given sufficient training, experience and possibly some divine assistance). The requirements appear to be that someone is still physically intact (ie the death is not from traumatic injury) and it's after a day or two, so before decay has really set in (and the etheric body is obviously still present), so it's typically death from an infectious disease, which a fully repaired etheric body could potentially successfully fight off, as well as repair any initial signs of decay in the body. Not that I am asking you to point me in the direction of any texts to learn about how to do it, but I was just curious as to whether, magically-speaking, resurrection is possible?

Thanks.
Mr. Crow

In response to your last query, there's a good example of resurrection by someone other than Jesus in 2 Kings 4:18-37, when Elisha brings the Shunnamite woman's son back to life.

It seems like a case of heat stroke. The boy has been (possibly) dead for less than a day. Elisha has to lay his full body upon the boy multiple times. It seems as if Elisha is actually transferring some of his etheric substance to the boy.

Elisha is one of the most flamboyant and overtly magical of the prophets; if you read about the other things he has done, it's pretty apparent he was no ordinary person, and could channel some sort of extra-human power. I doubt resurrection can be accomplished by anything less than a divine vehicle.

--Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: 4 questions

comments.title: Unitarian Universalists
comments.content:
Greetings this Monday evening,
In your various explorations of the magical history of the US have you or will you get to Unitarian Universalists? I recognize that they are more the religious side of things rather than the magical, but I know the Unity church came up at some point for its connections to magical movements, and the UU church seems to be a haven for various pagans who seek religious community. In my experience, the national body seems to be very aligned with left-wing political and media movements, and individual congregations mirror that to various extents. I've had similarly mixed experiences, from joyful connection to feeling ostracized for believing in magic and a real, tangible spiritual-realm, or shamed for having even somewhat centrist social/political views.
I've joked that I'm a Unitarian Diversalist (particularly inspired by your essay(s) on dissensus.) I'm curious if there is a clear historical reason for the strangeness within that community.
Thanks as always for your time and for this space.

replies.title: Re: Unitarian Universalists
replies.content: That's a long, complex story. The Unitarian Church has been aligned with liberal social causes since the 1820s, and gradually gave up any genuine religious content as that became unfashionable on the Left. In 1961, iirc, they engineered a merger with the Universalist Church, which was much less political, and basically devoured it -- there's next to no Universalism left in the UUA. The Gnostic church in which I'm a bishop was founded by three Universalist ministers who schismed because they didn't want anything to do with the merger. These days? To judge by the UU congregations I've visited, the UU church exists because some people have a fixed habit of going someplace to sing off key on Sunday mornings but have forgotten why.

replies.title: Re: Unitarian Universalists
replies.content: Unity doesn't have anything to do with the Unitarians.  It's one of the more successful and enduring New Thought churches, tracing its ancestry back to Emma Curtis Hopkins, and before her, to Mary Baker Eddy and Christian Science.  Unity was founded by Charles and Myrtle Filmore in 1889 in Kansas City, Missouri.  Like all the other New Thought churches, it built on Eddy's leading idea, that the material world has no reality or permanence, but in its present form is merely the product of human thought, riddled with errors and ignorance; and that it can be altered to some extent by changing one's thinking.  As our host has said in the past, this is basically "magic with the serial numbers filed off."  It makes considerable use of affirmations as a means of changing one's thinking.
